file.content:
# Build websockify-go: minimal WebSocket→TCP proxy (~5 MB static binary)
FROM golang:1.26-alpine AS websockify-builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY websockify/ .
# go mod tidy downloads deps and generates go.sum; -mod=mod allows updating go.sum at build time
RUN go mod tidy && CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o websockify .

FROM ubuntu:25.10

ARG SANDCASTLE_VERSION=dev

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# System tools
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    openssh-server sudo curl git tmux vim neovim \
    build-essential \
    jq ripgrep fd-find htop wget unzip ca-certificates net-tools iproute2 iputils-ping \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# GUI tools: TigerVNC (Xvnc = virtual X server + VNC server combined) and window manager.
# Xvnc sends the RFB banner immediately on connect, unlike x11vnc 0.9.17+ which waits
# for client data first (breaking websockify's server-speaks-first expectation).
# noVNC static files are served from the Rails app (public/novnc/), not from the sandbox.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    tigervnc-standalone-server openbox xterm xfonts-base xfonts-100dpi xfonts-75dpi \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# websockify-go: single static binary replaces python3-websockify (~50 MB → ~5 MB)
COPY --from=websockify-builder /build/websockify /usr/local/bin/websockify

# Google Chrome (amd64) or Chromium (arm64) — Google doesn't ship a Chrome deb for arm64
ARG TARGETARCH
RUN if [ "$TARGETARCH" = "amd64" ]; then \
      apt-get update \
      && wget -q https://dl.google.com/linux/direct/google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
      && apt-get install -y ./google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
      && rm google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
      && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*; \
    else \
      # Ubuntu ships chromium as a snap-only wrapper since 19.10 — unusable in containers.
      # Pull the real chromium .deb from Debian testing (arm64 only), pinned so nothing
      # else is silently upgraded from Debian.
      apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl gpg \
      && curl -fsSL https://ftp-master.debian.org/keys/archive-key-12.asc \
           | gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/debian-archive.gpg \
      && echo "deb [arch=arm64] http://deb.debian.org/debian testing main" \
           > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/debian-testing.list \
      && printf 'Package: *\nPin: release o=Debian\nPin-Priority: 100\n\nPackage: chromium chromium-common chromium-sandbox\nPin: release o=Debian\nPin-Priority: 500\n' \
           > /etc/apt/preferences.d/debian-chromium \
      && apt-get update \
      && apt-get install -y chromium \
      && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*; \
    fi

# Prefer IPv4 to avoid slow/broken IPv6 connections
RUN sed -i 's/#precedence ::ffff:0:0\/96  100/precedence ::ffff:0:0\/96  100/' /etc/gai.conf

# Docker CLI + daemon (Sysbox makes this safe)
# Use manual apt repo instead of get.docker.com convenience script —
# that script tries to install ca-certificates/curl which Ubuntu 25.10 already
# has at newer versions, causing "E: Packages were downgraded".
# Pin to 'noble' — Docker has no packages for Ubuntu 25.10 (questing) yet.
RUN install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings \
    && curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc \
    && chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc \
    && echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu noble stable" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list \
    && apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Pin runc to 1.1.x — runc 1.2+ added a procfs safety check that fails
# inside sysbox containers (sysbox-fs mounts /proc/sys as separate FUSE)
RUN RUNC_VERSION="v1.1.15" \
    && ARCH=$(dpkg --print-architecture) \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/releases/download/${RUNC_VERSION}/runc.${ARCH}" \
       -o /usr/bin/runc \
    && chmod +x /usr/bin/runc

# Docker Compose plugin
RUN mkdir -p /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/latest/download/docker-compose-linux-$(uname -m)" \
       -o /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

# Stage mise + Claude Code in /opt/sandcastle/bin (seeded to ~/.local/bin on first boot)
RUN mkdir -p /opt/sandcastle/bin \
    && curl https://mise.run | REDACTED sh \
    && curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash \
    && cp -L /root/.local/bin/claude /opt/sandcastle/bin/claude \
    && rm -rf /root/.local/share/claude /root/.local/bin/claude

# ttyd — web terminal (two ports: 7681=tmux, 7682=shell)
RUN ARCH=$(dpkg --print-architecture) \
    && TTYD_ARCH=$([ "$ARCH" = "arm64" ] && echo "aarch64" || echo "x86_64") \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/tsl0922/ttyd/releases/latest/download/ttyd.${TTYD_ARCH}" \
       -o /usr/local/bin/ttyd \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/ttyd

# Entire CLI — git observability for AI agent sessions (https://entire.io)
RUN ARCH="$(dpkg --print-architecture)" \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/latest/download/entire_linux_${ARCH}.tar.gz" \
       | tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin entire \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/entire

# Shell config: PATH, mise activation, aliases
RUN echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> /etc/bash.bashrc \
    && echo 'eval "$(mise activate bash)"' >> /etc/bash.bashrc \
    && echo "alias yolo='claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'" >> /etc/bash.bashrc

# Bake version into the image
RUN echo "${SANDCASTLE_VERSION}" > /etc/sandcastle-version

# SSH configuration (key-only auth) — use drop-in config for Ubuntu 25.10 compatibility
RUN mkdir -p /var/run/sshd /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d \
    && echo "PermitRootLogin no" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf \
    && echo "PubkeyAuthentication yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf \
    && echo "PasswordAuthentication no" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf

COPY entrypoint.sh /entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod +x /entrypoint.sh

COPY startchrome.sh /usr/local/bin/google-chrome
RUN chmod +x /usr/local/bin/google-chrome \
    && ln -s /usr/local/bin/google-chrome /usr/local/bin/startchrome

COPY tmux.conf /etc/tmux.conf
COPY gitconfig /etc/gitconfig

LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/thieso2/sandcastle"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="Sandcastle sandbox image — Ubuntu 25.10 with Docker-in-Docker, SSH, and dev tools"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace
EXPOSE 22 5900 6080 7681 7682

ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]

content.thinking:
Now I have a clearer picture. The Dockerfile runs as root (no USER directive), and the entrypoint runs as root. So the "Permission denied" on mkdir should not be happening for root... unless the home directory itself is not writable by root in the container's context.

Wait, I think I finally understand the problem. Let me trace through carefully:

1. Rails (running as UID 1000 in the `sandcastle-web` container) creates `/data/users/thies/home` and sets it to `chmod 0o777`
2. The sandbox container starts with Sysbox (user namespace). Sysbox maps container UID 0 to some high host UID, e.g., `231072`.


3. When `useradd -m -s /bin/bash thies` runs, it creates the user with UID 1000 and warns that the home directory already exists.
4. The `chown thies:thies /home/thies` command changes ownership from the Rails process's UID to the new user's UID 1000, which maps to a different host UID (~232072), effectively changing the host-level ownership.
5. After `chmod 755 /home/thies`, the directory is now owned by container UID 1000 with restrictive permissions, so root inside the container can no longer write to it.

Actually, wait—I need to reconsider how Sysbox handles this. The bind mount `/data/users/thies/home:/home/thies` points to a host directory owned by UID 1000 with mode 777. Inside the Sysbox container, UID translation happens at the syscall level, so the permissions and ownership behavior might be different than I initially thought.

When container root (UID 0, mapped to host UID 231072) tries to access this directory, it sees the owner as UID 65534 (the overflow UID for unmapped host UIDs), but the 777 mode allows write access anyway. After the `chown thies:thies` command inside the container, the host ownership changes to UID 232072 (container UID 1000's mapping). Then `chmod 755` restricts permissions, leaving only the owner with write access. Now container root (host UID 231072) can't write to this directory because it's not the owner and the mode doesn't grant write to others.

The key issue is that the bind mount's underlying filesystem is the host's ext4/btrfs, and the kernel performs permission checks using host UIDs. Host UID 231072 (container root) doesn't match the directory owner (host UID 232072), and the "other" bits of 755 don't include write permission, so the kernel denies the mkdir operation. This is the fundamental problem with Sysbox bind mounts—unlike normal Docker where container root is actually host root and can bypass permissions, here the unprivileged host UID can't write to a directory it doesn't own.

The solution is to delay the `chmod 755` until after root has finished all necessary setup operations like creating .ssh and .local/bin directories, or keep the bind-mounted directories at 777 to allow the container root to write. There's also the sshd StrictModes configuration to consider as a separate issue. The fix is straightforward: I need to move the chown and chmod operations to the very end, after all the directory creation and file seeding is complete. That way the home directory stays writable by root throughout the setup process, and only gets locked down to 755 after everything is in place. 33-34. Or alternatively, do the chown/chmod AFTER all the setup.

Actually, the cleanest fix is to reorder the entrypoint:
1. useradd
2. SSH setup (mkdir, set up keys) - before chown/chmod, so root can write
3. Seed tools (mkdir .local/bin, copy tools) - before chown/chmod
4. chown -R $USERNAME:$USERNAME /home/$USERNAME (now covers .ssh and .local)
5. chmod 755 /home/$USERNAME

Actually, we also need to be careful with the chown -R. Right now, line 47 does a recursive chown on just .local, while line 33 only chowns the directory itself without recursion.

Let me think through the proper sequence: create the user, then make .ssh and .local/bin directories as root while the home is still world-writable, seed the tools into those directories, then do a recursive chown of the entire home directory to the user, and finally set the home directory permissions to 755. The recursive chown should handle everything including the bind-mounted home directory from previous sessions, which is probably fine since the user should own their own files anyway. After that, I need to make sure the SSH directory gets 700 and the authorized_keys file gets 600 permissions. Setting up the local bin directory...

Now I need to consolidate the ownership changes—instead of chowning the SSH directory separately, I'll do a single recursive chown on the entire home directory after seeding the tools, which handles both .ssh and .local in one step. Let me trace through the revised order: user creation, SSH key setup without chown, workspace configuration, tool seeding, then the comprehensive home directory ownership and permissions fix.

I should update the entrypoint script in the sandbox image, but that's in the Dockerfile and requires a full rebuild cycle. The user mentioned "livepatch" though, so they might be looking for a quicker workaround—though I'm not sure how to apply a patch to the entrypoint when it's baked into the container at build time. For a true livepatch on the running system, I'd need to either rebuild the image on sandman after fixing the entrypoint locally and pushing it, or directly patch the running container—but the cleanest approach is to fix it locally, push the updated image to the registry, then pull it on sandman so new sandboxes get the corrected entrypoint. I can patch the sandbox image directly by creating a temporary container, copying the fixed entrypoint into it, and committing it back as the latest version—this way new containers will pick up the fix without needing a full rebuild.

Writing the entrypoint fix... Setting up SSH authorized keys and workspace permissions...

Now I'm copying mise and Claude tools into the user's local bin directory on first boot, then setting proper ownership and permissions on the home directory so SSH can access it correctly.

After that, I'm configuring git with the user's name and email if those environment variables were provided, writing them to the system-wide git config.

content.text:
Found it. The entrypoint does `chown $USERNAME:$USERNAME /home/$USERNAME && chmod 755 /home/$USERNAME` at line 33–34, then tries `mkdir -p ~/.local/bin` at line 41. With Sysbox user namespaces, container root maps to a non-privileged host UID — standard DAC applies on bind mounts, so root can no longer write into a `755` dir owned by someone else.

Fix: move all home-dir writes *before* the `chown/chmod`.

input.old_string:
# Set up SSH authorized keys (append if not already present, preserving
# any WeTTY keys that may have been injected for other sandboxes sharing
# this user's home directory via bind mount).
if [ -n "$SSH_KEY" ]; then
    SSH_DIR="/home/$USERNAME/.ssh"
    mkdir -p "$SSH_DIR"
    if [ -f "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" ]; then
        grep -qF "$SSH_KEY" "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" || echo "$SSH_KEY" >> "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    else
        echo "$SSH_KEY" > "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    fi
    chmod 700 "$SSH_DIR"
    chmod 600 "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "$SSH_DIR"
fi

# Ensure home directory ownership and permissions.
# The host may create bind-mounted home dirs with 777 so Sysbox-mapped root
# can write. Tighten to 755 here so sshd StrictModes is satisfied.
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME"
chmod 755 "/home/$USERNAME"

# Ensure workspace is accessible
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /workspace 2>/dev/null || true

# Seed mise + Claude Code into user's ~/.local/bin on first boot
USER_LOCAL_BIN="/home/$USERNAME/.local/bin"
mkdir -p "$USER_LOCAL_BIN"
for tool in mise claude; do
    if [ ! -f "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool" ] && [ -f "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" ]; then
        cp "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool"
    fi
done
chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME/.local"
input.new_string:
# Set up SSH authorized keys (append if not already present, preserving
# any WeTTY keys that may have been injected for other sandboxes sharing
# this user's home directory via bind mount).
if [ -n "$SSH_KEY" ]; then
    SSH_DIR="/home/$USERNAME/.ssh"
    mkdir -p "$SSH_DIR"
    if [ -f "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" ]; then
        grep -qF "$SSH_KEY" "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" || echo "$SSH_KEY" >> "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    else
        echo "$SSH_KEY" > "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    fi
    chmod 700 "$SSH_DIR"
    chmod 600 "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
fi

# Seed mise + Claude Code into user's ~/.local/bin on first boot.
# IMPORTANT: must run before chown/chmod of the home dir below.
# With Sysbox user-namespace mapping, container root maps to a non-privileged
# host UID, so standard DAC applies on bind mounts.  While home is still 777
# root can freely create dirs; after chmod 755 root can no longer write inside.
USER_LOCAL_BIN="/home/$USERNAME/.local/bin"
mkdir -p "$USER_LOCAL_BIN"
for tool in mise claude; do
    if [ ! -f "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool" ] && [ -f "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" ]; then
        cp "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool"
    fi
done

# Set correct ownership and permissions on the home directory.
# chown -R covers .ssh, .local, and anything else created above.
# chmod 755 is required by sshd StrictModes.
# The host creates bind-mounted home dirs with 777 so Sysbox-mapped root
# can write during setup (see above); tighten to 755 now that setup is done.
chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME"
chmod 755 "/home/$USERNAME"

# Ensure workspace is accessible
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /workspace 2>/dev/null || true

        grep -qF "$SSH_KEY" "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" || echo "$SSH_KEY" >> "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    else
        echo "$SSH_KEY" > "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    fi
    chmod 700 "$SSH_DIR"
    chmod 600 "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
fi

# Seed mise + Claude Code into user's ~/.local/bin on first boot.
# IMPORTANT: must run before chown/chmod of the home dir below.
# With Sysbox user-namespace mapping, container root maps to a non-privileged
# host UID, so standard DAC applies on bind mounts.  While home is still 777
# root can freely create dirs; after chmod 755 root can no longer write inside.
USER_LOCAL_BIN="/home/$USERNAME/.local/bin"
mkdir -p "$USER_LOCAL_BIN"
for tool in mise claude; do
    if [ ! -f "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool" ] && [ -f "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" ]; then
        cp "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool"
    fi
done

# Set correct ownership and permissions on the home directory.
# chown -R covers .ssh, .local, and anything else created above.
# chmod 755 is required by sshd StrictModes.
# The host creates bind-mounted home dirs with 777 so Sysbox-mapped root
# can write during setup (see above); tighten to 755 now that setup is done.
chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME"
chmod 755 "/home/$USERNAME"

# Ensure workspace is accessible
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /workspace 2>/dev/null || true
toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

USERNAME="${SANDCASTLE_USER:-sandbox}"
SSH_KEY="${SANDCASTLE_SSH_KEY:-}"

# Create user if not exists
if ! id "$USERNAME" &>/dev/null; then
    useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo,docker "$USERNAME"
    echo "$USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" >> /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle
    chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle
fi

# Set up SSH authorized keys (append if not already present, preserving
# any WeTTY keys that may have been injected for other sandboxes sharing
# this user's home directory via bind mount).
if [ -n "$SSH_KEY" ]; then
    SSH_DIR="/home/$USERNAME/.ssh"
    mkdir -p "$SSH_DIR"
    if [ -f "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" ]; then
        grep -qF "$SSH_KEY" "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" || echo "$SSH_KEY" >> "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    else
        echo "$SSH_KEY" > "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    fi
    chmod 700 "$SSH_DIR"
    chmod 600 "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "$SSH_DIR"
fi

# Ensure home directory ownership and permissions.
# The host may create bind-mounted home dirs with 777 so Sysbox-mapped root
# can write. Tighten to 755 here so sshd StrictModes is satisfied.
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME"
chmod 755 "/home/$USERNAME"

# Ensure workspace is accessible
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /workspace 2>/dev/null || true

# Seed mise + Claude Code into user's ~/.local/bin on first boot
USER_LOCAL_BIN="/home/$USERNAME/.local/bin"
mkdir -p "$USER_LOCAL_BIN"
for tool in mise claude; do
    if [ ! -f "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool" ] && [ -f "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" ]; then
        cp "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool"
    fi
done
chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME/.local"

# Configure git identity system-wide if provided
if [ -n "$USER_FULLNAME" ] || [ -n "$USER_EMAIL" ]; then
    {
        echo "[user]"
        [ -n "$USER_FULLNAME" ] && echo "    name = $USER_FULLNAME"
        [ -n "$USER_EMAIL" ] && echo "    email = $USER_EMAIL"
    } >> /etc/gitconfig
fi

# Generate SSH host keys if missing
ssh-keygen -A

# Resize /dev/shm to 2GB for Chrome. Docker's ShmSize HostConfig key is not
# supported by sysbox-runc, so we do it here instead. The runtime bind-mounts
# /dev/shm from a small constrained shm, so "remount,size=" fails; unmounting
# it and mounting a fresh tmpfs works.
umount /dev/shm 2>/dev/null || true
mount -t tmpfs -o size=2g,mode=1777 tmpfs /dev/shm 2>/dev/null || true

# Start Docker daemon in background (requires Sysbox runtime for isolated /var/lib/docker)
# Don't wait for it to be ready - users can check with `docker info` after SSH login
if command -v dockerd &>/dev/null && [ -e /dev/fuse ]; then
    # Match inner Docker bridge MTU to container's eth0 to avoid packet fragmentation
    ETH0_MTU=$(ip link show eth0 2>/dev/null | grep -oP 'mtu \K[0-9]+' || echo 1500)
    dockerd --storage-driver=overlay2 --mtu="$ETH0_MTU" &>/var/log/dockerd.log &
else
    echo "Note: Docker-in-Docker not available (requires sysbox-runc runtime)" >&2
fi

# Start virtual X + VNC server for browser access.
# Xvnc (TigerVNC) combines Xvfb and a VNC server in a single process and sends
# the RFB banner immediately on connect — required for websockify compatibility.
# x11vnc 0.9.17+ waits for client data before sending the banner, deadlocking
# with websockify which also waits for the server to speak first.
# Both processes run as $USERNAME (not root) for proper display ownership.
VNC_ENABLED="${SANDCASTLE_VNC_ENABLED:-1}"
VNC_GEOMETRY="${SANDCASTLE_VNC_GEOMETRY:-1280x900}"
VNC_DEPTH="${SANDCASTLE_VNC_DEPTH:-24}"

if command -v Xvnc &>/dev/null && [ "$VNC_ENABLED" = "1" ]; then
    touch /var/log/xvnc.log /var/log/openbox.log
    chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /var/log/xvnc.log /var/log/openbox.log
    su -s /bin/bash "$USERNAME" -c \
        "Xvnc :99 -rfbport 5900 -SecurityTypes None -AlwaysShared -geometry ${VNC_GEOMETRY} -depth ${VNC_DEPTH} &>/var/log/xvnc.log &"
    # Start Openbox window manager once the display is ready
    if command -v openbox &>/dev/null; then
        su -s /bin/bash "$USERNAME" -c \
            'DISPLAY=:99 openbox &>/var/log/openbox.log &'
    fi
    # Start websockify-go: proxies WebSocket connections to Xvnc on port 6080.
    # noVNC static files are served from the Rails app (public/novnc/), not here.
    touch /var/log/websockify.log
    chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /var/log/websockify.log
    websockify -addr :6080 -target localhost:5900 -url /websockify &>/var/log/websockify.log &
    # Export DISPLAY for all SSH sessions via PAM environment
    echo "DISPLAY=:99" >> /etc/environment
fi

# Start ttyd web terminals (run as sandbox user)
# Port 7681: tmux session (persistent, re-attaches; unlimited clients)
# Port 7682: plain login shell (one client at a time)
if command -v ttyd &>/dev/null; then
    touch /var/log/ttyd-tmux.log /var/log/ttyd-shell.log
    chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /var/log/ttyd-tmux.log /var/log/ttyd-shell.log
    su -s /bin/bash "$USERNAME" -c \
        "ttyd -W -m 0 -p 7681 tmux new-session -A -s main &>/var/log/ttyd-tmux.log &"
    su -s /bin/bash "$USERNAME" -c \
        "ttyd -W -m 1 -p 7682 bash -l &>/var/log/ttyd-shell.log &"
fi

# Start SSH daemon in foreground
exec /usr/sbin/sshd -D -e

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(sandbox): move home-dir writes before chown/chmod in entrypoint

With Sysbox user-namespace mapping, container root maps to a
non-privileged host UID, so standard DAC applies on bind mounts.
The old order did chown+chmod 755 on /home/$USER before seeding
~/.local/bin, which then denied root write access and caused
8x "mkdir: Permission denied" errors on startup.

Fix: perform all mkdir/cp operations inside the home dir while it is
still 777, then do chown -R + chmod 755 at the end. Also consolidate
the separate `.ssh` chown into the final chown -R over the whole dir.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" -- images/sandbox/entrypoint.sh

content.text:
Done. Here's what was happening and why:

**Root cause:** Sysbox user namespaces map container root (UID 0) to a high, non-privileged host UID (e.g., 231072). When the home dir is a bind mount from the host, the kernel applies normal DAC rules using *host* UIDs. After `chmod 755 /home/thies` owned by container UID 1000 (host UID ~232072), host UID 231072 (container root) has no write access — it's just an unprivileged "other". All subsequent `mkdir` calls inside home failed.

**Fix:** Reordered so all writes into home (`.ssh` setup, `.local/bin` seeding) happen while the dir is still `777`, then `chown -R` + `chmod 755` at the end. Also consolidated the separate `.ssh` chown into the single recursive chown.

The image is already patched on sandman — new sandboxes will work immediately. Push when ready.

class RouteManager
  DATA_DIR = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR", "/data")
  DYNAMIC_DIR = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "dynamic")
  NETWORK_NAME = "sandcastle-web"

  TCP_PORT_MIN = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN", 3000).to_i
  TCP_PORT_MAX = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX", 3099).to_i
  TCP_PORT_RANGE = (TCP_PORT_MIN..TCP_PORT_MAX)

  TRAEFIK_STATIC_CONFIG = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG", File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "traefik.yml"))
  TRAEFIK_CONTAINER = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONTAINER", "sandcastle-traefik")

  class Error < StandardError; end

  def add_route(sandbox:, domain: nil, port: 8080, mode: "http")
    raise Error, "Sandbox is not running" unless sandbox.status == "running"

    route = Route.transaction do
      if mode == "tcp"
        public_port = allocate_tcp_port
        sandbox.routes.create!(mode: "tcp", port: port, public_port: public_port)
      else
        sandbox.routes.create!(mode: "http", domain: domain, port: port)
      end
    end

    if mode == "tcp"
      ensure_tcp_entrypoint(route.public_port)
    end

    ensure_network
    connect_to_network(sandbox)
    write_config(sandbox)

    route
  rescue ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid => e
    raise Error, e.message
  end

  def remove_route(route:)
    sandbox = route.sandbox
    route.destroy!

    if sandbox.routes.reload.any?
      write_config(sandbox)
    else
      delete_config(sandbox)
      disconnect_from_network(sandbox)
    end
  end

  def remove_all_routes(sandbox:)
    return unless sandbox.routed?

    sandbox.routes.destroy_all
    delete_config(sandbox)
    disconnect_from_network(sandbox)
  end

  def suspend_routes(sandbox:)
    return unless sandbox.routed?

    delete_config(sandbox)
    disconnect_from_network(sandbox)
  end

  def reconnect_routes(sandbox:)
    return unless sandbox.routed?

    ensure_network
    connect_to_network(sandbox)
    write_config(sandbox)
  end

  def sync_all_configs
    FileUtils.mkdir_p(DYNAMIC_DIR)

    active_ids = Sandbox.active.running.joins(:routes).distinct.pluck(:id).to_set

    # Remove stale config files
    Dir.glob(File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "sandbox-*.yml")).each do |path|
      id = File.basename(path, ".yml").delete_prefix("sandbox-").to_i
      unless active_ids.include?(id)
        File.delete(path)
        Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: removed stale config #{File.basename(path)}")
      end
    end

    # Regenerate configs for active routed sandboxes
    Sandbox.active.running.joins(:routes).distinct.includes(:user, :routes).find_each do |sandbox|
      ensure_network
      connect_to_network(sandbox)
      write_config(sandbox)
    end
  end

  def write_rails_config(host:)
    FileUtils.mkdir_p(DYNAMIC_DIR)

    # Build host list: main host + optional alternative hostnames
    hosts = [ host ]
    alt_hostnames = ENV["SANDCASTLE_ALT_HOSTNAMES"].to_s.split(",").map(&:strip).reject(&:empty?)
    hosts += alt_hostnames

    host_rules = hosts.map { |h| "`#{h}`" }.join(", ")
    rule = "Host(#{host_rules})"

    config = {
      "http" => {
        "routers" => {
          "rails-http" => {
            "rule" => rule,
            "service" => "rails",
            "entryPoints" => [ "web" ]
          },
          "rails-https" => {
            "rule" => rule,
            "service" => "rails",
            "entryPoints" => [ "websecure" ],
            "tls" => tls_config
          }
        },
        "services" => {
          "rails" => {
            "loadBalancer" => {
              "servers" => [ { "url" => "http://sandcastle-web:80" } ]
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }

    File.write(File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "rails.yml"), config.to_yaml)
    write_tls_config
  end

  private

  SELFSIGNED_MODES = %w[selfsigned mkcert].freeze

  def write_tls_config
    tls_path = File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "tls.yml")

    if SELFSIGNED_MODES.include?(ENV["SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE"])
      # Traefik-perspective path (referenced inside tls.yml)
      cert_dir = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_TLS_CERT_DIR", "/data/certs")
      # Rails-perspective path (where we can actually write files)
      local_cert_dir = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "certs")

      case ENV["SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE"]
      when "selfsigned" then ensure_selfsigned_cert(local_cert_dir)
      when "mkcert"     then ensure_mkcert_cert(local_cert_dir)
      end

      certs = [ { "certFile" => "#{cert_dir}/cert.pem", "keyFile" => "#{cert_dir}/key.pem" } ]

      if custom_cert_configured?
        certs << { "certFile" => "/data/certs/custom-cert.pem", "keyFile" => "/data/certs/custom-key.pem" }
      end

      File.write(tls_path, { "tls" => { "certificates" => certs } }.to_yaml)
    else
      File.delete(tls_path) if File.exist?(tls_path)
    end
  end

  def ensure_selfsigned_cert(cert_dir)
    cert_path = File.join(cert_dir, "cert.pem")
    key_path  = File.join(cert_dir, "key.pem")
    return if File.exist?(cert_path) && File.exist?(key_path)

    host = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_HOST", "localhost")
    Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: generating self-signed certificate for #{host}")

    key  = OpenSSL::PKey::RSA.generate(4096)
    cert = OpenSSL::X509::Certificate.new
    cert.version    = 2
    cert.serial     = OpenSSL::BN.rand(128)
    cert.subject    = OpenSSL::X509::Name.parse("/CN=#{host}")
    cert.issuer     = cert.subject
    cert.public_key = key.public_key
    cert.not_before = Time.now
    cert.not_after  = Time.now + 10 * 365 * 24 * 60 * 60

    ef = OpenSSL::X509::ExtensionFactory.new
    ef.subject_certificate = cert
    ef.issuer_certificate  = cert

    san = host.match?(/\A[\d.]+\z/) ? "IP:#{host}" : "DNS:#{host}"
    san += ",IP:127.0.0.1,DNS:localhost"
    cert.add_extension(ef.create_extension("subjectAltName", san))
    cert.add_extension(ef.create_extension("basicConstraints", "CA:FALSE", true))
    cert.add_extension(ef.create_extension("keyUsage", "digitalSignature,keyEncipherment", true))
    cert.sign(key, OpenSSL::Digest::SHA256.new)

    FileUtils.mkdir_p(cert_dir)
    File.write(key_path,  key.to_pem,  perm: 0o600)
    File.write(cert_path, cert.to_pem, perm: 0o644)
    Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: self-signed certificate written to #{cert_path}")
  end

  def ensure_mkcert_cert(cert_dir)
    cert_path = File.join(cert_dir, "cert.pem")
    key_path  = File.join(cert_dir, "key.pem")
    return if File.exist?(cert_path) && File.exist?(key_path)

    host = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_HOST", "localhost")
    Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: generating mkcert certificate for #{host}")

    # Store CA alongside the certs so it survives container restarts
    caroot = ENV.fetch("MKCERT_CAROOT", cert_dir)
    FileUtils.mkdir_p(cert_dir)

    env = { "CAROOT" => caroot }
    system(
      env,
      "mkcert",
      "-cert-file", cert_path,
      "-key-file",  key_path,
      host, "*.#{host}", "localhost", "127.0.0.1", "::1"
    ) or raise Error, "mkcert certificate generation failed for #{host}"

    ca_source = File.join(caroot, "rootCA.pem")
    ca_dest   = File.join(cert_dir, "rootCA.pem")
    FileUtils.cp(ca_source, ca_dest) if File.exist?(ca_source) && File.expand_path(ca_source) != File.expand_path(ca_dest)

    Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: mkcert certificate written to #{cert_path}")
  end

  def custom_cert_configured?
    cert_path = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "certs", "custom-cert.pem")
    key_path = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "certs", "custom-key.pem")
    File.exist?(cert_path) && File.exist?(key_path)
  end

  def config_path(sandbox)
    File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "sandbox-#{sandbox.id}.yml")
  end

  def write_config(sandbox)
    FileUtils.mkdir_p(DYNAMIC_DIR)

    routes = sandbox.routes.reload
    http_routers = {}
    http_services = {}
    tcp_routers = {}
    tcp_services = {}

    routes.each do |route|
      if route.http?
        key = "sandbox-#{sandbox.id}-r#{route.id}"
        http_routers[key] = {
          "rule" => "Host(`#{route.domain}`)",
          "service" => key,
          "entryPoints" => [ "websecure" ],
          "tls" => tls_config
        }
        http_services[key] = {
          "loadBalancer" => {
            "servers" => [ { "url" => "http://#{sandbox.full_name}:#{route.port}" } ]
          }
        }
      else
        key = "sandbox-#{sandbox.id}-tcp-r#{route.id}"
        tcp_routers[key] = {
          "rule" => "HostSNI(`*`)",
          "entryPoints" => [ "tcp-#{route.public_port}" ],
          "service" => key
        }
        tcp_services[key] = {
          "loadBalancer" => {
            "servers" => [ { "address" => "#{sandbox.full_name}:#{route.port}" } ]
          }
        }
      end
    end

    config = {}
    if http_routers.any?
      config["http"] = { "routers" => http_routers, "services" => http_services }
    end
    if tcp_routers.any?
      config["tcp"] = { "routers" => tcp_routers, "services" => tcp_services }
    end

    File.write(config_path(sandbox), config.to_yaml)
  end

  def tls_config
    if SELFSIGNED_MODES.include?(ENV["SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE"])
      {}
    else
      { "certResolver" => "letsencrypt" }
    end
  end

  def delete_config(sandbox)
    path = config_path(sandbox)
    File.delete(path) if File.exist?(path)
  end

  def allocate_tcp_port
    used = Route.where(mode: "tcp").lock.pluck(:public_port).to_set
    TCP_PORT_RANGE.find { |p| !used.include?(p) } ||
      raise(Error, "No TCP ports available (pool #{TCP_PORT_MIN}–#{TCP_PORT_MAX} exhausted)")
  end

  def ensure_tcp_entrypoint(port)
    return unless File.exist?(TRAEFIK_STATIC_CONFIG)

    config = YAML.safe_load(File.read(TRAEFIK_STATIC_CONFIG)) || {}
    entry_key = "tcp-#{port}"

    entry_points = config["entryPoints"] ||= {}
    return if entry_points.key?(entry_key)

    entry_points[entry_key] = { "address" => ":#{port}" }
    File.write(TRAEFIK_STATIC_CONFIG, config.to_yaml)
    Rails.logger.info("RouteManager: added Traefik entrypoint #{entry_key}, restarting #{TRAEFIK_CONTAINER}")

    container = Docker::Container.get(TRAEFIK_CONTAINER)
    container.restart
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    Rails.logger.warn("RouteManager: Traefik container #{TRAEFIK_CONTAINER} not found, skipping restart")
  rescue Errno::ENOENT, Errno::EACCES => e
    Rails.logger.warn("RouteManager: could not update Traefik static config: #{e.message}")
  end

  def ensure_network
    Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    Docker::Network.create(NETWORK_NAME, "Driver" => "bridge")
  end

  def connect_to_network(sandbox)
    return unless sandbox.container_id.present?

    network = Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
    container = Docker::Container.get(sandbox.container_id)

    # Check if already connected
    networks = container.json.dig("NetworkSettings", "Networks") || {}
    return if networks.key?(NETWORK_NAME)

    network.connect(sandbox.container_id)
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    raise Error, "Failed to connect container to network: #{e.message}"
  end

  def disconnect_from_network(sandbox)
    return unless sandbox.container_id.present?

    network = Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
    network.disconnect(sandbox.container_id)
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    # Network or container already gone
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError
    # Container may not be connected
  end
end

file.content:
class Route < ApplicationRecord
  belongs_to :sandbox

  validates :domain, presence: true, uniqueness: true,
    format: { with: /\A[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9.-]*[a-z0-9])?\.[a-z]{2,}\z/i, message: "must be a valid domain" },
    if: :http?
  validates :public_port, presence: true, uniqueness: true, if: :tcp?
  validates :mode, inclusion: { in: %w[http tcp] }
  validates :port, presence: true, inclusion: { in: 1..65535 }

  def http? = mode == "http"
  def tcp?  = mode == "tcp"

  def url
    http? ? "https://#{domain}" : nil
  end
end

content.thinking:
Now I see it! Traefik is binding `0.0.0.0:3000-3099->3000-3099/tcp`! So Traefik IS listening on port 3000 (and 3001-3099). Not OrbStack. The `lsof` output showed OrbStack, but that might be OrbStack's overlay for Docker port forwarding.

So Traefik IS listening on ports 3000-3099, and those should be the TCP routes. The SSH connection goes to `::1:3000` → Traefik → which should forward to the sandbox's port 22.


The `kex_exchange_identification: Connection closed by remote host` error tells me Traefik is accepting the connection but immediately closing it. That's likely because there's no dynamic config file defining a TCP router for port 3000, or the sandbox container itself doesn't exist—I'm seeing `thies-cosmic-eagle` and `thies-quantum-panther` locally, but no `thies-hase` container that the user is trying to connect to.

I'm realizing `hase` in the CLI config is actually a SERVER name pointing to `https://hase.sandcastle.rocks`, not a local sandbox name, so `hase.dev.sand` would be a sandbox on the dev server instead. Looking at what's running locally, there's no `thies-hase` container, which means the connection attempt is failing because the target doesn't exist.

The user needs to create a sandbox named `hase` on their local dev Sandcastle instance, but I should check the Traefik dynamic config to understand how the local instance routes TCP connections to sandboxes.

content.thinking:
Now I see the full picture:

1. There's a TCP route in the DB: `sandbox=cosmic-eagle port=22 public=3000`
2. Traefik's static config only has `web` and `websecure` entrypoints — no `tcp-3000` entrypoint!
3. The `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` method should have added it to `traefik.yml` and restarted Traefik, but it didn't

So the problem is: the TCP route was created in the DB (for sandbox `cosmic-eagle`, port 22 → public port 3000), but the Traefik static config doesn't have the `tcp-3000` entrypoint. 

The port binding in docker-compose maps 3000-3099 to the container, so Traefik accepts connections on port 3000, but without the corresponding entrypoint configured, it just closes them immediately since it has no routing rules for that traffic. The `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` method loads the Traefik static config, checks if the TCP entrypoint already exists, and if not, adds it with the specified port and writes the updated config back to the file.

I'm noticing a potential mismatch though—the code writes to `/data/traefik/traefik.yml` based on the `SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG` environment variable, but inside the Traefik container, the config file is actually located at `/etc/traefik/traefik.yml`. This suggests the Rails app might be modifying a config file that Traefik isn't actually reading from.

content.text: The TCP route exists (`cosmic-eagle → port 22 → public 3000`) but the `tcp-3000` entrypoint is missing from Traefik's static config. That's why Traefik binds the port (via Docker compose) but immediately drops connections — it has no router for that entrypoint.

Now I see everything clearly:

1. The dynamic config for sandbox-1 has:
   - HTTP route for `hase.dev.sand` → `thies-cosmic-eagle:8080`
   - TCP route on entrypoint `tcp-3000` → `thies-cosmic-eagle:22`

3. The `ensure_tcp_entrypoint(3000)` method should have added `tcp-3000` to `traefik.yml` and restarted Traefik, but it didn't work (or it was not called, or Traefik wasn't restarted properly).
   
So I need to add the `tcp-3000` entrypoint to the Traefik static config and restart it. I can either call the Rails method directly via the runner or manually edit the config file — I'll go with the Rails runner approach since it's cleaner and handles the restart automatically.

content.text: The dynamic config is correct (`tcp-3000` router → `thies-cosmic-eagle:22`) but `tcp-3000` is missing from the Traefik **static** config. `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` didn't stick. Fix it now:

input.command:
docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails runner "
  Route.where(mode: 'tcp').pluck(:public_port).each do |port|
    RouteManager.new.send(:ensure_tcp_entrypoint, port)
  end
  puts 'done'
" 2>&1 | grep -v ContainerSyncJob | grep -v ActiveJob | grep -v Performing | grep -v Performed | grep -v Startup | grep -v Traefik


**What happened:** `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` wrote the static config and called `container.restart`, but the Rails app was looking at `SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONTAINER = "sandcastle-traefik"` which matched (confirmed by the 3-second uptime). Connected. `LANDED: thies-bold-lion` — we're inside the sandbox.

**Root cause chain:**
1. `traefik.yml` was created with `root:root 644` — the Rails process (non-root) couldn't write it
2. `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` rescues `EACCES` silently → TCP entrypoint never got added to Traefik's static config
3. Traefik bound the port via Docker compose but had no entrypoint config for it → immediate close

**Fix needed in code** — the `traefik.yml` file needs to be created writable by the Rails user from the start. Let me check how it's initialized and fix that:

content.thinking:
So the installer creates `traefik.yml` using a heredoc (`cat > "$TRAEFIK_DIR/traefik.yml"`) as root (or whatever user runs the installer). The file is owned by the installer user, not the Rails user.

For local dev, there's no installer - the file is created differently. Looking at the named Docker volume `sandcastle_traefik-data`, the file must be created by some init step or by the Traefik container itself.

But the real fix for both local dev and production is to ensure the `traefik.yml` file is writable by the Rails user. There are a few approaches:


1. **In the installer**: After creating `traefik.yml`, I could chmod it to be world-writable or chown it to the Rails user
2. **In Rails**: At startup, fix permissions on the static config file - but this won't work since Rails can't chmod a file it doesn't own and isn't running as root
3. **Use a different approach**: Instead of modifying the static config file, use Traefik's API to add entrypoints, though that's more complex
4. **Fix the volume permissions in docker-compose**: Use an init container or entrypoint that handles the ownership before Rails starts

Actually, the cleanest solution is to pre-configure the entire port range (3000-3099) as TCP entrypoints in Traefik's static config during installation, then let the dynamic config handle routing only the ports that are actually in use. This avoids any runtime modifications to the static config file entirely. I'm thinking through the implications of having 100 pre-configured entrypoints...

Traefik handles many entrypoints fine since they're just port declarations, though it's a bit wasteful for a small deployment. The real advantage is no permission issues and no need to restart Traefik when adding new TCP routes. Alternatively, I could stick with the current approach but fix permissions by ensuring the static config file has the right permissions when Rails initializes it, or add `chmod 666` to the installer after creating the file.

The safer production fix is to set proper user ownership in the installer—if the Rails container runs as `rails:1000`, I can `chown 1000:1000 traefik.yml` so Rails can write to it without making it world-writable. For local dev, I'd need to handle the shared volume permissions differently, either through docker-compose configuration or a startup script.

Now I'm checking the docker-compose setup to see where the `traefik.yml` file gets created and why it ends up owned by root in the local dev environment.

file.content:
services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.3
    container_name: sandcastle-traefik
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
      - "8443:443"
      - "3000-3099:3000-3099"
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/etc/traefik
      - ./.cert:/certs:ro
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web
    depends_on:
      init-traefik:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  init-traefik:
    image: busybox
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/data
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - |
        mkdir -p /data/dynamic
        chown -R 220568:220568 /data
        if [ ! -f /data/traefik.yml ]; then
          cat > /data/traefik.yml << 'EOF'
        entryPoints:
          web:
            address: ":80"
          websecure:
            address: ":443"
        providers:
          file:
            directory: /etc/traefik/dynamic
            watch: true
        log:
          level: INFO
        EOF
          cat > /data/dynamic/rails.yml << 'EOFCONFIG'
        http:
          routers:
            rails-http:
              rule: "Host(`${DEV_HOST}`)"
              service: rails
              entryPoints:
                - web
            rails-https:
              rule: "Host(`${DEV_HOST}`)"
              service: rails
              entryPoints:
                - websecure
              tls: {}
          services:
            rails:
              loadBalancer:
                servers:
                  - url: "http://sandcastle-web:80"
        EOFCONFIG
        fi

  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql
      - ./docker/postgres/init-databases.sh:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init-databases.sh:ro
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: sandcastle
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      POSTGRES_DB: sandcastle_development
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U sandcastle -d sandcastle_development"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  init-data:
    image: busybox
    volumes:
      - sandcastle-data:/data
    command: sh -c "mkdir -p /data/users /data/sandboxes && chown -R 220568:220568 /data"

  web:
    image: sandcastle:dev
    build:
      context: .
      target: development
      args:
        BUILD_VERSION: "dev"
        BUILD_GIT_SHA: ""
        BUILD_GIT_DIRTY: ""
        BUILD_DATE: ""
    container_name: sandcastle-web
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-0}"
    volumes:
      # Mount source code for live reloading
      - .:/rails
      # Persist gems in volume (faster than bind mount)
      - bundle-data:/usr/local/bundle
      # Persist node modules
      - node-modules:/rails/node_modules
      # Share Docker socket
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      # Data directory
      - sandcastle-data:/data
      # Traefik config
      - traefik-data:/data/traefik
    networks:
      - default
      - sandcastle-web
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: development
      PORT: 80
      BINDING: 0.0.0.0
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: ${DEV_HOST}
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: selfsigned
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_CERT_DIR: /certs
      SANDCASTLE_TERMINAL_URL: https://${DEV_HOST}:8443
      SANDCASTLE_VNC_URL: https://${DEV_HOST}:8443
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: "3000"
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: "3099"
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: ./bin/dev
    depends_on:
      init-data:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      init-traefik:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  worker:
    image: sandcastle:dev
    build:
      context: .
      target: development
      args:
        BUILD_VERSION: "dev"
        BUILD_GIT_SHA: ""
        BUILD_GIT_DIRTY: ""
        BUILD_DATE: ""
    container_name: sandcastle-dev-worker
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-0}"
    volumes:
      # Mount source code for live reloading
      - .:/rails
      # Persist gems in volume (faster than bind mount)
      - bundle-data:/usr/local/bundle
      # Share Docker socket
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      # Data directory
      - sandcastle-data:/data
      # Traefik config
      - traefik-data:/data/traefik
    networks:
      - default
      - sandcastle-web
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: development
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: ${DEV_HOST}
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: selfsigned
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_CERT_DIR: /certs
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: "3000"
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: "3099"
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: ["bundle", "exec", "rake", "solid_queue:start"]
    depends_on:
      init-data:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  migrate:
    image: sandcastle:dev
    build:
      context: .
      target: development
    volumes:
      - .:/rails
      - bundle-data:/usr/local/bundle
    command: ["./bin/rails", "db:prepare"]
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: development
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_USER: thies
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_EMAIL: thieso@gmail.com
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_PASSWORD: tubu
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_SSH_KEY: "ssh-ed25519 REDACTED thieso@gmail.com"
      SANDCASTLE_SKIP_PASSWORD_CHANGE: "true"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

networks:
  sandcastle-web:
    external: true

volumes:
  bundle-data:
  node-modules:
  pgdata:
  sandcastle-data:
  traefik-data:

file.content:
services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.3
    container_name: sandcastle-traefik
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
      - "8443:443"
      - "3000-3099:3000-3099"
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/etc/traefik
      - ./.local/certs:/data/certs:ro
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web
    depends_on:
      init-traefik:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  init-traefik:
    image: busybox
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/data
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - |
        mkdir -p /data/dynamic /data/certs
        chown -R 220568:220568 /data
        if [ ! -f /data/traefik.yml ]; then
          cat > /data/traefik.yml << 'EOF'
        entryPoints:
          web:
            address: ":80"
          websecure:
            address: ":443"
        providers:
          file:
            directory: /etc/traefik/dynamic
            watch: true
        log:
          level: INFO
        EOF
        fi

  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql
      - ./docker/postgres/init-databases.sh:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init-databases.sh:ro
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: sandcastle
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      POSTGRES_DB: sandcastle_production
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U sandcastle -d sandcastle_production"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  init-data:
    image: busybox
    volumes:
      - sandcastle-data:/data
    command: sh -c "mkdir -p /data/users /data/sandboxes && chown -R 220568:220568 /data"

  web:
    image: sandcastle:local
    build:
      context: .
      args:
        BUILD_VERSION: "${BUILD_VERSION:-dev}"
        BUILD_GIT_SHA: "${BUILD_GIT_SHA:-local}"
        BUILD_GIT_DIRTY: "${BUILD_GIT_DIRTY:-false}"
        BUILD_DATE: "${BUILD_DATE:-$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)}"
    container_name: sandcastle-web
    command: ["./bin/rails", "server", "-b", "0.0.0.0", "-p", "80"]
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-0}"
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      - sandcastle-data:/data
      - traefik-data:/data/traefik
      - ./.local/certs:/data/traefik/certs
    networks:
      - default
      - sandcastle-web
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      RAILS_SHOW_DETAILED_ERRORS: "true"
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: dev.sand
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: mkcert
      SANDCASTLE_TERMINAL_URL: https://dev.sand:8443
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: "3000"
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: "3099"
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      init-data:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  worker:
    image: sandcastle:local
    build:
      context: .
      args:
        BUILD_VERSION: "${BUILD_VERSION:-dev}"
        BUILD_GIT_SHA: "${BUILD_GIT_SHA:-local}"
        BUILD_GIT_DIRTY: "${BUILD_GIT_DIRTY:-false}"
        BUILD_DATE: "${BUILD_DATE:-$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)}"
    container_name: sandcastle-worker
    command: ["bundle", "exec", "rake", "solid_queue:start"]
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-0}"
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      - sandcastle-data:/data
      - traefik-data:/data/traefik
    networks:
      - default
      - sandcastle-web
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: dev.sand
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: mkcert
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: "3000"
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: "3099"
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      init-data:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  migrate:
    image: sandcastle:local
    command: ["./bin/rails", "db:prepare"]
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE:-deadbeef}
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_USER: thies
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_EMAIL: thieso@gmail.com
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_PASSWORD: tubu
      SANDCASTLE_ADMIN_SSH_KEY: "ssh-ed25519 REDACTED thieso@gmail.com"
      SANDCASTLE_SKIP_PASSWORD_CHANGE: "true"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

networks:
  sandcastle-web:
    external: true

volumes:
  pgdata:
  sandcastle-data:
  traefik-data:

content.thinking:
Found the bug! In `init-traefik` (both `docker-compose.dev.yml` and `docker-compose.local.yml`):

```yaml
mkdir -p /data/dynamic
chown -R 220568:220568 /data
if [ ! -f /data/traefik.yml ]; then
  cat > /data/traefik.yml << 'EOF'
  ...
  EOF
fi
```

The issue is the ORDER:
1. `mkdir -p /data/dynamic` - creates directory
2. `chown -R 220568:220568 /data` - chowns everything to UID 220568


3. Then `cat > /data/traefik.yml` runs as root (since busybox runs as root), creating the file with root ownership even though the directory was already chowned to 220568. The fix is to move the `chown -R` command to after the file creation, or add a `chmod 666 /data/traefik.yml` to make it writable.

The simpler approach is reordering the commands so the ownership change happens last and covers everything including the newly created file. I should also check the installer script since it creates `traefik.yml` as root without chowning it to the Rails user—I'll need to add the same ownership fix there or use `chmod 666` in both places.

content.text: Found the bug. In `init-traefik`, the `chown -R 220568:220568 /data` runs **before** `cat > /data/traefik.yml`, so the file is created by root after the chown and ends up `root:root 644`. Fix both compose files by moving `chown` to after the file creation:

        providers:

    image: traefik:v3.3
    container_name: sandcastle-traefik

    ports:

      - "8443:443"
      - "3000-3099:3000-3099"
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/etc/traefik
      - ./.cert:/certs:ro
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web
    depends_on:
      init-traefik:
        condition: service_completed_successfully

  init-traefik:
    image: busybox
    volumes:
      - traefik-data:/data
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - |
        mkdir -p /data/dynamic
        chown -R 220568:220568 /data
        if [ ! -f /data/traefik.yml ]; then
          cat > /data/traefik.yml << 'EOF'
        entryPoints:
          web:
            address: ":80"
          websecure:
            address: ":443"
        providers:
          file:
            directory: /etc/traefik/dynamic
            watch: true
        log:
          level: INFO
        EOF
          cat > /data/dynamic/rails.yml << 'EOFCONFIG'
        http:
          routers:
            rails-http:
              rule: "Host(`${DEV_HOST}`)"
              service: rails
              entryPoints:
                - web
            rails-https:
              rule: "Host(`${DEV_HOST}`)"
              service: rails
              entryPoints:
                - websecure
              tls: {}
          services:
            rails:
              loadBalancer:
                servers:
                  - url: "http://sandcastle-web:80"
        EOFCONFIG
        fi

  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:

  init-data:

    volumes:

      # Share Docker socket

content.text: Now commit:

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(compose): chown traefik-data after creating traefik.yml, not before

init-traefik ran chown -R 220568:220568 before cat > traefik.yml,
so the file was created by root (busybox) after the chown and ended
up root:root 644. Rails (UID 220568) couldn't write to it, causing
ensure_tcp_entrypoint to silently fail (EACCES rescued), leaving
Traefik without the tcp-3000 entrypoint so all TCP-route SSH
connections were immediately dropped.

Fix: move chown to after the file-creation block.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" -- docker-compose.local.yml docker-compose.dev.yml
input.description: Commit the chown order fix

content.text:
SSH is working (`LANDED: thies-bold-lion`). Here's the full chain that was broken:

1. `init-traefik` ran `chown -R 220568:220568 /data` **before** `cat > /data/traefik.yml` — file created by busybox root after the chown → `root:root 644`
2. Rails (UID 220568) called `ensure_tcp_entrypoint(3000)` → `File.write` threw `EACCES` → rescued silently with just a warn log → `traefik.yml` never updated
3. Traefik had the port bound via Docker compose (`3000-3099:3000-3099`) but no entrypoint config → TCP accepted, then immediately dropped

file.content:
services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.6.8
    container_name: sandcastle-traefik
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
      - "${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN:-3000}-${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX:-3099}:${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN:-3000}-${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX:-3099}"
    volumes:
      - /data/traefik/traefik.yml:/etc/traefik/traefik.yml
      - /data/traefik/dynamic:/data/dynamic:ro
      - /data/traefik/acme.json:/data/acme.json
      - /data/traefik/certs:/data/certs:ro
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web

  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - /data/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql
      - ./docker/postgres/init-databases.sh:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init-databases.sh:ro
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: sandcastle
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      POSTGRES_DB: sandcastle_production
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U sandcastle -d sandcastle_production"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web

  web:
    image: ghcr.io/thieso2/sandcastle:latest
    container_name: sandcastle-web
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-988}"
    cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      - /data:/data
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: ${SANDCASTLE_HOST}
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: ${SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE:-letsencrypt}
      SANDCASTLE_ALT_HOSTNAMES: ${SANDCASTLE_ALT_HOSTNAMES:-}
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      GITHUB_CLIENT_ID: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_ID:-}
      GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET: ${GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET:-}
      GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID: ${GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID:-}
      GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET: ${GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET:-}
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: ${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN:-3000}
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: ${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX:-3099}
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web

  worker:
    image: ghcr.io/thieso2/sandcastle:latest
    container_name: sandcastle-worker
    command: ["./bin/jobs"]
    group_add:
      - "${DOCKER_GID:-988}"
    cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      - /data:/data
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT}
      SANDCASTLE_HOST: ${SANDCASTLE_HOST}
      SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR: /data
      SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE: ${SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE:-letsencrypt}
      SANDCASTLE_ALT_HOSTNAMES: ${SANDCASTLE_ALT_HOSTNAMES:-}
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN: ${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MIN:-3000}
      SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX: ${SANDCASTLE_TCP_PORT_MAX:-3099}
      SANDCASTLE_TRAEFIK_CONFIG: /data/traefik/traefik.yml
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      migrate:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web

  migrate:
    image: ghcr.io/thieso2/sandcastle:latest
    command: ["./bin/rails", "db:prepare"]
    environment:
      RAILS_ENV: production
      SECRET_KEY_BASE: ${SECRET_KEY_BASE}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_PRIMARY_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_DETERMINISTIC_KEY}
      AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT: ${AR_ENCRYPTION_KEY_DERIVATION_SALT}
      DB_HOST: postgres
      DB_USER: sandcastle
      DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-sandcastle}
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - sandcastle-web

networks:
  sandcastle-web:
    external: true

content.thinking:
Now I can see the production setup clearly:

**Production `docker-compose.yml`:**
- Traefik mounts `traefik.yml` as a bind mount from the HOST: `/data/traefik/traefik.yml:/etc/traefik/traefik.yml`
- Rails also mounts `/data:/data`, so `traefik.yml` is at `/data/traefik/traefik.yml`
- Both use the same host file — no separate named volume
- There's NO `init-traefik` container in production (unlike local dev)

**Installer:**
- Creates `traefik.yml` at line 947/994/1024 using `cat > "$TRAEFIK_DIR/traefik.yml"` 


- The installer only chowns the `dynamic` subdirectory at line 1099, leaving `traefik.yml` itself with whatever ownership the installer process had (likely root)
- No chmod is applied to `traefik.yml` after creation, so it retains default permissions
- Rails runs as UID 220568 inside the container and needs write access to `traefik.yml` on the host, but if the installer ran as root, the file would be owned by root and inaccessible to the sandcastle user

Let me check the installer logs to confirm who actually runs it and what ownership `traefik.yml` ends up with on sandman.

content.text: In production, `traefik.yml` is a host bind mount (not a named volume). The installer chowns `dynamic/` but let me check if `traefik.yml` itself gets chowned:

content.thinking:
Confirmed: on sandman, `/sandcastle/data/traefik/traefik.yml` is `root:root 644`. Rails (UID 220568 sandcastle user) can't write to it.

The installer at line 1099 only chowns the `dynamic` subdirectory, not `traefik.yml` itself.

So production has the same bug:
1. `traefik.yml` is `root:root 644`
2. Rails can't write to it when `ensure_tcp_entrypoint` runs
3. TCP routes would silently fail on production too

Fix needed:


I need to update the installer to chown the traefik.yml file to the sandcastle user after creating it, and immediately fix the permissions on sandman by changing ownership to UID 220568. Let me look up the exact SANDCASTLE_UID value on sandman and apply both fixes.

http:

      service: rails
      entryPoints:
        - websecure
      tls:
        certResolver: letsencrypt
  services:
    rails:
      loadBalancer:
        servers:
          - url: "http://sandcastle-web:80"
TEOF
  fi
